title: Alexander's Gamble
subtitle: The Battle of Gaugamela, 331 BC
tagline: Darius III had a million soldiers. He leveled the battlefield himself so his chariots had room to run. Alexander had 47,000. He attacked anyway.
villain_mandate: Darius III must feel genuinely, massively unbeatable. One million soldiers. Scythed chariots. War elephants. A leveled plain chosen specifically to maximize Persian advantages. Alexander's victory must feel against-impossible-odds and cost him nearly everything.

scenes.title: The Leveled Plain
scenes.narration: 331 BC. Darius III, King of Persia, had learned from his predecessor's mistake at Issus — the terrain had been too narrow. So he chose a vast open plain near Gaugamela in modern Iraq, and he had it leveled. His engineers smoothed the earth for miles so nothing would obstruct his scythed chariots. One million soldiers. Two hundred scythed chariots. Fifteen war elephants. He built the perfect battlefield. Then he waited for Alexander.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. The vast plain of Gaugamela at dawn — perfectly flat, stretching to the horizon, deliberately prepared. Persian army filling it: one million soldiers in formation, the scale incomprehensible. Scythed chariots at the front. War elephants in the rear. Darius's royal tent in the center, massive, golden. Alexander's scouts on a ridge in the foreground looking down at this. 16:9.

scenes.title: Darius's Advantage
scenes.narration: Darius's generals told him this was the day Alexander died. His left flank alone outnumbered Alexander's entire army. His center contained the elite Persian Immortals and Greek mercenaries — the finest soldiers money could buy. His scythed chariots could cut through infantry formations like wheat. His cavalry on the wings would fold Alexander's flanks and encircle whatever was left. The plan was perfect. He had prepared for everything.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. Persian military council — Darius on his throne surrounded by generals, maps showing the overwhelming Persian numbers. Each Persian advantage shown: the scythed chariot blades rotating, the Immortals in formation, the cavalry wings extending far beyond Alexander's flanks. Darius's expression: complete, justified confidence. He has accounted for everything. 16:9.

scenes.title: Alexander Sees the Gap
scenes.narration: Alexander spent the night before the battle studying the Persian line. His generals wanted to attack at night — Parmenion argued the darkness would negate Persian numbers. Alexander refused. 'I will not steal my victory,' he said. He studied the Persian line until he saw it: Darius was so confident his wings would fold Alexander's flanks that he had left a gap — a small gap — between his center and his right cavalry. That gap was the battle.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. Alexander at night — young, intense, studying maps and reports about the Persian line. His generals around him arguing for a night attack. His finger on the map at the point between the Persian center and right cavalry. The gap that nobody else sees yet. His expression: he has it. The night sky over the Persian camp fires in the background. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Oblique Advance
scenes.narration: October 1, 331 BC. Alexander advanced his line at an oblique angle — not straight forward. Drifting right. This forced Darius to extend his left flank to match, stretching the Persian line thin. The scythed chariots charged. Macedonian infantry opened lanes and closed behind them — the chariots ran through empty corridors and were cut down from behind. The Persian greatest weapon: gone in minutes.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. The Battle of Gaugamela in motion — Alexander's line advancing at an angle, drifting right. The Persian left flank stretching to match. Scythed chariots charging, Macedonian infantry opening deliberate lanes, the chariots crashing through — then Macedonian sarissa spears closing the trap. The chariots destroyed within their own charge. 16:9.

scenes.title: Alexander Charges the King
scenes.narration: The Persian left stretched to stop Alexander's drift. It stretched too far. The gap opened. Alexander wheeled the Companion Cavalry — the finest heavy cavalry in the world — and drove them directly into the gap at full gallop. Not at the Persian army. At the Persian king. Darius III, from his chariot, watched Alexander coming for him personally. At the head of a cavalry wedge. Directly at his position.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. THE CHARGE — Alexander at the head of the Companion Cavalry, wedge formation, galloping through the gap in the Persian line directly toward Darius. Dust and chaos around them. Darius on his royal chariot in the center, watching the cavalry wedge coming through the gap. His guards forming around him. The distance closing. 16:9.

scenes.title: The King Breaks
scenes.narration: Darius fled. The greatest king in the world, on his royal chariot, with one million soldiers behind him, turned and ran. His chariot got stuck. He switched to a horse. He rode east and did not stop. When the Persian center saw their king's royal standard moving away from the battle, the center broke. One million soldiers began to dissolve. The battle was decided the moment Darius chose to live.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. Darius's chariot — the royal chariot getting stuck in the chaos, Darius switching to a horse in undignified haste. His royal standard moving away from the battle. The Persian center soldiers seeing the standard retreat and their faces changing — from battle posture to the beginning of fear. The ripple of collapse spreading. 16:9.

scenes.title: Parmenion Is Losing
scenes.narration: But Alexander's left was in trouble. General Parmenion's flank was being overrun — Persian cavalry had broken through and were attacking the Macedonian baggage train. Parmenion sent an urgent message: he needed help. Alexander had Darius in reach. He could have chased the king, captured him, ended the war in one afternoon. He turned back to save his general.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. The left flank in chaos — Parmenion's troops being overrun by Persian cavalry. A messenger reaching Alexander mid-pursuit, his hand pointed back to the left flank. Alexander's expression: the choice. The Persian king is ahead of him, fleeing. His general is behind him, failing. He turns. 16:9.

scenes.title: Alexander Holds the Field
scenes.narration: Alexander drove his cavalry into the Persian breakthrough on the left flank. Brutal, close-quarters fighting — his Companions against the Persian cavalry that had already broken through. They held. The Macedonian line stabilized. Darius was gone. His army was broken. But the Persian cavalry that had broken through had also escaped. The war would continue — but Persia had no throne to come back to.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. Alexander's cavalry fighting the Persian breakthrough on the left — brutal, desperate, grinding. Then stabilization. The Macedonian line holding. Darius gone in the dust to the east. The battlefield: hundreds of thousands of soldiers, most of them Persian, in retreat. The scale of the victory visible only from above. 16:9.

scenes.title: The End of the Persian Empire
scenes.narration: Gaugamela was the end. Babylon surrendered without a fight. Persepolis — the Persian capital, the heart of the empire — fell three months later. Darius was murdered by his own generals before Alexander could capture him. Alexander, age twenty-five, stood in the ruins of the greatest empire on Earth and was declared King of Asia. He had broken a million soldiers with forty-seven thousand.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. Alexander riding into Persepolis — the Persian capital, monumental columns, the Treasury. The city surrendering. Darius's empty throne. Alexander, twenty-five years old, standing in the throne room of an empire that had existed for two hundred years. The scale of what just ended. 16:9.

scenes.title: 47,000 Against a Million
scenes.narration: Darius prepared the perfect battlefield. He outnumbered Alexander more than twenty to one. His plan was flawless — until Alexander found the one gap and drove his cavalry directly at the king. Every army has a critical point. Every plan has one assumption it can't survive losing. Darius assumed Alexander wouldn't find it in time. He found it on the first day. Subscribe to Gods & Glory for more battles that changed history.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. Final card — the plain of Gaugamela at sunset, empty now. The Persian camp abandoned. In the distance: Alexander's standard moving east, toward Babylon. One man. Forty-seven thousand soldiers. The largest empire in human history, ended. 16:9.

viral_hook: Darius had a million soldiers and a perfectly prepared battlefield. Alexander had 47,000. Alexander looked at the Persian line all night and found one gap. One.
highlight_note: Scene 6 — Darius flees. One million soldiers behind him. He chose to live. That choice ended the Persian Empire.
youtube_title: Alexander's Gamble: 47,000 vs 1,000,000 | Battle of Gaugamela | Gods & Glory
lesson: The largest army is only as strong as its commander's nerve. When Darius broke, a million soldiers broke with him.
